data.content: Now I'll create the `.template.config/template.json` file and update the source files to use template parameters for the project name/namespace.

@@ -1,0 +1,15 @@
+{
+  "$schema": "http://json.schemastore.org/template",
+  "author": "Team",
+  "classifications": ["Test", "xUnit"],
+  "identity": "TeamTestProject",
+  "name": "Team Test Project",
+  "shortName": "team-tests",
+  "tags": {
+    "language": "C#",
+    "type": "project"
+  },
+  "sourceName": "MyTests",
+  "preferNameDirectory": true
+}
+

<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net9.0</TargetFramework>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <IsPackable>false</IsPackable>
    <TreatWarningsAsErrors>true</TreatWarningsAsErrors>
  </PropertyGroup>

    <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk" Version="17.11.0" />

      <IncludeAssets>runtime; build; native; contentfiles; analyzers; buildtransitive</IncludeAssets>
      <PrivateAssets>all</PrivateAssets>
    </PackageReference>
  </ItemGroup>
</Project>

{

}

data.content:
The template is working correctly. Here's what was created:

**File:** `test-output/MyTests/.template.config/template.json`

- **Name:** "Team Test Project"
- **Short name:** `team-tests`
- **`sourceName: "MyTests"`** — automatically replaces `MyTests` with the project name in filenames, `.csproj`, and namespaces.

Your team can use it with:
```
dotnet new install ./test-output/MyTests
dotnet new team-tests -n MyNewTests
```
